stories.headline: INC rally on EDSA swells to over 15,000; four arrested, follow-up march permitted for July 1
stories.summary: Thousands of Iglesia ni Cristo members gathered without a permit along EDSA and White Plains Avenue in Quezon City starting before dawn on June 30, in a show of support for Senator Rodante Marcoleta ahead of an expected plunder case. Police estimated the crowd grew past 15,000 by evening, snarling Metro Manila traffic after protesters used buses to block lanes including the EDSA Carousel busway. The Philippine National Police said four people were arrested, including a bus driver accused of pinning down an officer with a bus, and that two officers were injured.
stories.context: Marcoleta, a former Senate Blue Ribbon Committee chair who led the flood-control corruption probe, faces a possible plunder case over an alleged 75 million pesos in undeclared 2025 campaign donations; INC, an influential bloc-voting group, has called the case "selective justice."
stories.watch_for: Quezon City granted INC a permit for a follow-up rally on July 1, 8 a.m. to 8 p.m., along White Plains Avenue to Temple Drive, and the Ombudsman is expected to formally file plunder charges against Marcoleta this week.

stories.headline: AFP reports sharp rise in Chinese vessels near disputed West Philippine Sea shoals
stories.summary: The Armed Forces of the Philippines said it counted 44 Chinese coast guard and navy vessels across four West Philippine Sea features during the week of June 23-29, up from 17 the previous week. AFP spokesperson for the West Philippine Sea, Rear Admiral Roy Vincent Trinidad, said the largest concentration was near Bajo de Masinloc, with smaller numbers near Ayungin Shoal, Escoda Shoal, and the Pag-asa Islands.
stories.context: The Philippines and China have overlapping claims in the West Philippine Sea; the AFP publishes weekly vessel counts, which have ranged as high as 82 in a single week this year.

stories.headline: Belgian deputy PM visits Manila in first such trip in 33 years, eyes trade and ASEAN cooperation
stories.summary: Belgian Deputy Prime Minister and Foreign Affairs Minister Maxime Prevot arrived in Manila on June 30 for a three-day visit, described by the Department of Foreign Affairs as the first by a Belgian foreign minister in 33 years, coinciding with the 80th anniversary of Belgium-Philippine relations. He met Philippine Foreign Affairs Secretary Theresa Lazaro, with both sides proposing expanded cooperation in trade, investment, maritime affairs, and labor mobility, and discussing the Philippines' 2026 ASEAN chairmanship.
stories.context: Belgium is the Philippines' 30th-largest trading partner, with bilateral trade valued at about 480 million dollars in 2025.

stories.headline: Taal Volcano logs two minor eruptions in a day; alert level unchanged
stories.summary: The Philippine Institute of Volcanology and Seismology recorded two phreatomagmatic eruptions at Taal Volcano's main crater on June 30: the first lasted four minutes and produced a plume about 450 meters high, while a second around 2:34 p.m. lasted roughly four and a half minutes and sent ash and steam about 1,200 meters into the air before drifting southwest. Phivolcs said Taal remains under Alert Level 1, meaning low-level unrest with no mandatory evacuation required.
stories.context: Phreatomagmatic eruptions, driven by magma interacting with water, have recurred at Taal since 2021 and are considered typical behavior under Alert Level 1.

stories.headline: PAGASA says low-pressure area off Masbate likely to become tropical cyclone Henry within a day
stories.summary: PAGASA said a low-pressure area near San Pascual, Masbate had a high chance of developing into a tropical cyclone within 24 hours of its 3 a.m. June 30 position, either while crossing Southern Luzon and the Visayas or after emerging over the West Philippine Sea. If it forms, the system would be locally named Henry, the Philippines' eighth tropical cyclone of 2026. The bureau said it was already bringing scattered rain to Metro Manila, Calabarzon, Bicol, and the Visayas, with 50 to 100 millimeters of rain possible in Mindoro, Marinduque, Romblon, Aklan, and Capiz.
stories.watch_for: PAGASA said Tropical Cyclone Wind Signal No. 1 may be raised over Bicol, Quezon, Batangas, Mindoro, Marinduque, and Romblon once the system intensifies, with the system expected to emerge over the West Philippine Sea around July 1.

stories.headline: GCash parent Mynt files for record 92.3-billion-peso IPO
stories.summary: Mynt Inc., operator of e-wallet GCash, filed a registration statement with the Securities and Exchange Commission for an initial public offering of up to 8.03 billion common shares on the Philippine Stock Exchange, with an indicative maximum price of 10 pesos a share. At full size including the overallotment option, the offer could raise as much as 92.3 billion pesos, which would make it the largest IPO in Philippine history. Mynt reported 79.8 billion pesos in 2025 revenue and 17.2 billion pesos in net income, with about 39.1 million monthly active users.
stories.watch_for: The company is targeting a listing in the fourth quarter of 2026, subject to SEC review and market conditions.

stories.headline: Philippine trade deficit widens to 5.48 billion dollars in May on import surge
stories.summary: The Philippine Statistics Authority reported the trade-in-goods deficit widened to 5.48 billion dollars in May, up 50.5 percent from a year earlier and the fourth straight month of year-on-year widening, though narrower than April's revised 6.40 billion dollars. Imports rose 21.9 percent to 13.36 billion dollars while exports grew 7.6 percent to 7.87 billion dollars. An economist cited by the Inquirer attributed the gap largely to rising imports of electronics components and intermediate goods used in export production, plus a 35.6 percent jump in mineral fuel imports.
stories.context: The cumulative trade deficit for January to May 2026 reached 25.24 billion dollars, up 25 percent from the same period last year.
stories.watch_for: An economist cited in the report said the deficit could remain above 5 billion dollars through the third quarter of 2026.

stories.headline: BSP projects June inflation slowed to 6.0-7.0 percent, still above target
stories.summary: The Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas said its nowcast puts June 2026 headline inflation within a 6.0 to 7.0 percent range, compared with May's actual 6.8 percent and April's three-year-high of 7.2 percent, and still well above the central bank's 2.0-4.0 percent target band. The BSP attributed downward pressure to lower prices for rice, meat, and oil, offset by higher electricity rates and vegetable prices. The central bank also raised its full-year 2026 inflation forecast to 6.4 percent from 6.3 percent.
stories.context: The BSP's policy rate currently stands at 4.75 percent after two hikes in 2026; Governor Eli Remolona has signaled the central bank could call an off-cycle meeting if needed.
stories.watch_for: The Philippine Statistics Authority is due to release official June inflation data on July 7, 2026, with the next regular Monetary Board meeting set for August 27.

stories.headline: BPI permanently scraps fees on InstaPay and PESONet transfers
stories.summary: Bank of the Philippine Islands said it is permanently removing fees on InstaPay and PESONet fund transfers to other banks and e-wallets, effective July 1, across its app, online and mobile banking, and BanKo and BizKo platforms, with no daily cap on free InstaPay transfers. BPI previously charged 10 pesos per InstaPay transfer and 50 pesos per PESONet transfer.
stories.context: The move follows a June 17 circular from the Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas lifting a nearly five-year moratorium on changes to interbank transfer fees and directing banks toward market-based pricing.

stories.headline: Globe Telecom launches Starlink direct-to-mobile satellite service after NTC approval
stories.summary: The National Telecommunications Commission approved Globe Telecom's commercial rollout of Starlink direct-to-mobile satellite connectivity, which Globe says makes it the first telecom in the Philippines, and in Southeast Asia, to offer the service commercially. It targets areas outside terrestrial mobile coverage, initially supporting voice, messaging, and data on Android LTE smartphones with active Globe SIMs and a clear view of the sky. Pricing starts at 99 pesos for prepaid and TM subscribers, while higher-tier postpaid subscribers get it free.
stories.context: Globe piloted the satellite service for disaster response after the magnitude 7.8 earthquake that struck South Cotabato, Sultan Kudarat, and Sarangani, serving more than 150,000 customers during that emergency.

stories.headline: DOLE approves largest-ever Metro Manila minimum wage hike of 85 pesos
stories.summary: The National Capital Region wage board approved an 85-peso increase to the daily minimum wage, the largest such hike the board has approved, Labor Secretary Francis Tolentino said. The non-agriculture rate rises from 695 to 780 pesos, while the rate for agriculture and small retail, service, and manufacturing firms rises from 658 to 743 pesos. DOLE estimates the hike directly benefits about 1.1 million minimum-wage earners in Metro Manila.
stories.watch_for: The increase takes effect in two tranches: 60 pesos starting July 19, 2026, and an additional 25 pesos in January 2027.

stories.headline: US envoys meet Qatari mediators in Doha as Iran denies agreeing to direct talks with US
stories.summary: US Middle East envoy Steve Witkoff and senior adviser Jared Kushner traveled to Doha on June 30 to meet Qatari mediators, including Qatar's prime minister, as part of efforts to maintain a fragile ceasefire after days of US-Iran strikes around the Strait of Hormuz. President Trump said on social media that Iran had requested a meeting in Doha, but Iran's foreign ministry spokesperson denied any bilateral meeting with US officials was scheduled "at any level," saying Tehran would send only a technical delegation.
stories.context: The talks follow roughly four days of mutual strikes around the Strait of Hormuz that paused around June 29; the June 17 memorandum covers frozen Iranian funds, sanctions relief on oil exports, and administration of the strait.

stories.headline: Venezuela earthquake death toll passes 1,700 as rescue hopes fade six days on
stories.summary: The confirmed death toll from the magnitude 7.2 and 7.5 earthquakes that struck Venezuela's Caribbean coast on June 24 passed 1,700, with more than 5,000 people injured and tens of thousands still missing or unaccounted for, according to Venezuelan officials. More than 600 aftershocks have been recorded, including a magnitude 4.6 tremor on June 29. About 30,000 Venezuelan emergency workers and 2,700 foreign rescue personnel, including a French civil security regiment, are involved in search and recovery efforts, while electricity has been restored to 90 percent of La Guaira state.

stories.headline: WHO links over 1,300 excess deaths in Europe to ongoing record heatwave
stories.summary: The World Health Organization said more than 1,300 excess deaths across Europe have been linked to a heatwave underway since June 21, Director-General Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus said. Record temperatures were recorded in Slovakia (41 degrees Celsius), Czechia (41.9 degrees, breaking its prior national record), and Hungary (41.8 degrees), with at least 130 million people across Central and Eastern Europe experiencing temperatures above 35 degrees Celsius on June 29. Red heat warnings were issued for 22 Italian cities, while wildfires hit Croatia, Albania, and Bosnia and Herzegovina.
stories.context: Tedros said Europe is warming at roughly twice the global average, making it the fastest-warming continent.
stories.watch_for: Forecasters expect another heat surge from July 5 affecting France, Spain, Germany, Italy, Switzerland, and parts of the UK.

stories.headline: Putin acknowledges fuel shortages as Ukrainian drone strikes hit more Russian refineries
stories.summary: Russian President Vladimir Putin acknowledged fuel shortages and said there were "queues at petrol stations" across the country, calling for "systemic measures" to counter Ukrainian drone strikes that have hit several oil refineries, including in the Krasnodar and Yaroslavl regions. Most Russian regions have imposed limits on fuel sales, with some gas stations reporting queues of up to 12 hours and others closing. Targeted facilities also include the Norsk refinery, Russia's fourth largest, which suspended operations after an attack near Kstovo.
stories.context: Ukraine has intensified strikes on Russian energy infrastructure as part of a campaign to cut Moscow's oil export revenue and pressure the Kremlin toward negotiations; analysts say the disruption is expected to affect Russia's July-August wheat harvest season.
